feedback.text: I'm very much in favour of the Digital Fairness Act, however there is one thing I think it should also cover. There is already some regulation regarding video games in the act, but one major problem in modern gaming is not covered. In recent times it has become a reoccurring problem for publishers to simply revoke access to video games the user has already paid for. There are hundreds of games that have becomes completely unplayable because of this and are now lost to time. Video games are a form of art. Imagine if Leonardo da Vinci burned all his paintings. That would be a major loss of history. And in a couple centuries these video games will be that exact history. But instead of preserving them, they are being destroyed. There is already an EU citizen initiative called "Stop Destroying Videogames" that addresses this exact issue and is currently sitting at over 1.4 million signatures. This is in my opinion a perfect fit for the Digital Fairness Act, which covers many similar consumer rights issues. I would be very happy if it covers this. I do not want to force publishers to give support to games till the end of time, but simply to leave the games playable, for example by allowing users to host private servers. Sincerely, Junis Wagner
